structures.core_meaning: Permanent truths, habits, routines.
structures.primary_communicative_function: Stating general truths, describing daily life.
structures.when_to_use: With adverbs of frequency, general facts, schedules.
structures.key_variations: always, usually, often, sometimes, never.
structures.essential_vocabulary_slots: Routine verbs (wake up, get, go, have, start, finish).
structures.common_learner_mistakes: Missing -s, using present continuous for habits.
structures.chunk_compatibility: "I usually get up at 7.", "She never eats meat.", "Water boils at 100 degrees."
examples: The sun rises in the east.
He drinks coffee every morning.
Do you play tennis on Sundays?

structures.core_meaning: Action happening at the moment of speaking.
structures.when_to_use: With now, right now, at the moment; temporary situations.
structures.key_variations: Time markers; negative: not + verb-ing; questions.
structures.common_learner_mistakes: Using with stative verbs (I am wanting), omitting be.
structures.chunk_compatibility: "I'm working at the moment.", "It's raining.", "What are you doing?"
examples: She is cooking dinner now.
They are not listening.
Are you waiting for someone?

structures.core_meaning: Fixed future plan (social/professional).
structures.primary_communicative_function: Talking about definite plans in near future.
structures.when_to_use: When time and place are decided (diary future).
structures.key_variations: Going to vs Present Continuous for plans.
structures.essential_vocabulary_slots: Verbs of movement/activity (meeting, going, seeing, having, leaving).
structures.common_learner_mistakes: Forgetting time marker, using for general future without arrangement.
structures.chunk_compatibility: "I'm meeting her tomorrow.", "We're flying to Rome next week.", "He's having dinner with friends tonight."
examples: She's starting a new job on Monday.
What are you doing this weekend?
I'm not working next Friday.

structures.core_meaning: Finished actions at a specific past time.
structures.when_to_use: With yesterday, last night, ago, in 2010, when I was a child.
structures.key_variations: Regular -ed, irregular forms; negatives with didn't; questions with Did.
structures.essential_vocabulary_slots: Common past verbs (went, did, had, saw, ate, got, took).
structures.common_learner_mistakes: Using present after did (Did you went?), forgetting time marker for clear past.
structures.chunk_compatibility: "I saw a movie last night.", "She didn't come.", "When did you arrive?"
examples: We moved here five years ago.
He forgot his keys.
They didn't enjoy the party.

structures.core_meaning: Action in progress at a specific past time, background activity.
structures.when_to_use: When another action happened, longer action in past.
structures.key_variations: While/as; interrupted by Past Simple; all morning/day.
structures.common_learner_mistakes: Using for short completed events, forgetting was/were.
structures.chunk_compatibility: "I was sleeping when you called.", "What were you doing at 8pm?"
examples: At 9 o'clock I was working.
She was cooking while he was watching TV.
They weren't listening during the meeting.

structures.core_meaning: Past habit or state no longer true.
structures.when_to_use: To talk about discontinued habits/states.
structures.key_variations: Negative: didn't use to; Question: Did you use to...?
structures.essential_vocabulary_slots: Past activities, states (live, smoke, play, be).
structures.common_learner_mistakes: Use to in present context, writing "use to" in affirmative.
structures.chunk_compatibility: "I used to play the piano.", "She didn't use to like coffee.", "Did you use to live here?"
examples: My dad used to smoke.
This building used to be a cinema.
I didn't use to enjoy studying.

structures.core_meaning: Experience without specifying when.
structures.primary_communicative_function: Asking and talking about ever/never experiences.
structures.when_to_use: With ever, never, before, superlatives (the best I have ever ...).
structures.key_variations: Have you ever ...?, She's never + past participle.
structures.common_learner_mistakes: Adding a specific time (I have ever been to Paris in 2015).
structures.chunk_compatibility: "Have you ever tried sushi?", "I've never been to Asia.", "It's the best movie I've ever seen."
examples: I have never ridden a horse.
Has she ever met a celebrity?
We have visited London twice.

structures.core_meaning: Recent events, result, expectation.
structures.when_to_use: Just (very recent), already (sooner than expected), yet (expected, in questions/negatives).
structures.pattern: [Subject] + have/has + just/already + past participle; (negative) haven't/hasn't + past participle + yet
structures.key_variations: Yet at end of sentence.
structures.essential_vocabulary_slots: Verbs of completion (finish, eat, see, arrive).
structures.common_learner_mistakes: Yet in positive statements, word order of just/already (before participle).
structures.chunk_compatibility: "I've just finished.", "She's already left.", "Have you eaten yet?"
examples: I've just seen Paul.
We haven't decided yet.
He has already paid the bill.

structures.core_meaning: Action/state continuing from past to present.
structures.primary_communicative_function: Saying how long something has been true.
structures.when_to_use: With verbs that can continue (live, work, know, have, be).
structures.key_variations: for + period; since + point in time. Present Perfect Continuous possible with action verbs.
structures.common_learner_mistakes: Using present simple (I live here since 2010), mixing for and since.
structures.chunk_compatibility: "I've known her for ten years.", "He's worked here since 2018.", "How long have you been here?"
examples: I have lived in this city since 2015.
She has had this car for two months.
How long have you known each other?

structures.core_meaning: Past before past.
structures.primary_communicative_function: Showing sequence of two past events.
structures.when_to_use: When one past action happened before another past action.
structures.pattern: [Subject] + had + [past participle] + when/by the time/before + Past Simple
structures.key_variations: Already, just, never with Past Perfect.
structures.common_learner_mistakes: Using Past Perfect without second past action, overuse when order is clear.
structures.chunk_compatibility: "When I arrived, the train had already left.", "She had never seen snow before she moved to Canada."
examples: I had finished my work before the meeting started.
He realized he had lost his wallet.
By 10 pm, they had already gone to bed.

structures.core_meaning: Prediction based on opinion (will) vs evidence (going to).
structures.when_to_use: Use going to when you see something now; will for general opinion.
structures.pattern: I think + will; Look at... + going to.
structures.key_variations: Might for uncertain predictions.
structures.common_learner_mistakes: Using will when there is clear evidence.
structures.chunk_compatibility: "I think it will rain." vs "Look! It's going to rain."
examples: I think Brazil will win the match.
She's going to have a baby. (seen her belly)
The traffic is terrible. We're going to be late.

structures.core_meaning: Timetabled future events.
structures.when_to_use: For fixed timetables (transport, movies, classes).
structures.key_variations: The train leaves at 6 pm; What time does the show start?
structures.common_learner_mistakes: Using will for timetables (The train will leave at 6) unnatural.
structures.chunk_compatibility: "The bus leaves at 8.", "My flight arrives at noon."
examples: The meeting starts at 9 am tomorrow.
What time does the film begin?
School finishes on 15th June.

structures.core_meaning: Action in progress at a future time.
structures.primary_communicative_function: Describing ongoing future activity, polite enquiries about plans.
structures.when_to_use: At this time tomorrow, Will you be using...? (polite)
structures.key_variations: By this time next week; might be + -ing.
structures.common_learner_mistakes: Using for simple future events.
structures.chunk_compatibility: "I'll be waiting outside.", "This time tomorrow I'll be lying on the beach."
examples: Don't call at 8; I'll be eating dinner.
Will you be passing the supermarket?
Next month we'll be living in Madrid.

structures.structure: be going to vs Present Continuous for plans
structures.core_meaning: Both for plans; going to more intention, PC more fixed arrangement.
structures.primary_communicative_function: Distinguishing between decided intention and definite plan.
structures.when_to_use: Going to for intention (I've decided), PC for diary arrangement (specific place/time).
structures.pattern: I'm going to travel next month. / I'm travelling to Berlin on Friday.
structures.key_variations: Both acceptable in many contexts.
structures.common_learner_mistakes: Using PC for strong intention without arrangement, using will for everything.
structures.chunk_compatibility: "I'm going to start a course." vs "I'm starting the course on Monday."
examples: I'm going to learn Italian (intention). I'm starting lessons on Tuesday (arrangement).
We're going to buy a new car. We're seeing the dealer tomorrow.
She's going to invite them. She's having a party on Saturday.

structures.core_meaning: General truth, scientific fact.
structures.primary_communicative_function: Talking about things that always happen.
structures.when_to_use: Cause-effect that is always true.
structures.key_variations: When instead of if.
structures.common_learner_mistakes: Using will in both clauses.
structures.chunk_compatibility: "If you heat water, it boils.", "If I sleep late, I feel tired."
examples: If you mix blue and yellow, you get green.
When it rains, the streets get wet.
If I don't eat breakfast, I get hungry.

structures.core_meaning: Real/possible future condition and result.
structures.when_to_use: To discuss plans, warnings, promises.
structures.key_variations: Will can be replaced by can, might, imperative; unless.
structures.common_learner_mistakes: Using will in the if-clause, confusing with zero.
structures.chunk_compatibility: "If it rains, I'll take an umbrella.", "I'll call you if I'm late.", "If you don't hurry, we'll miss the bus."
examples: If you study hard, you will pass the exam.
She will be angry if you forget her birthday.
If I see him, I'll tell him.

structures.core_meaning: Unreal, imaginary, or unlikely present/future situation.
structures.when_to_use: To talk about things that are not true or very unlikely.
structures.key_variations: If I were you, I'd... (advice). Could/might instead of would.
structures.common_learner_mistakes: Using would in if-clause, using present for unreal.
structures.chunk_compatibility: "If I had more money, I'd travel.", "If I were you, I'd talk to her.", "What would you do if you won the lottery?"
examples: If I knew the answer, I would tell you.
She would be happier if she lived near her family.
If he were here, he'd help us.

structures.structure: Third conditional (If + had + past participle, would have + past participle)
structures.core_meaning: Imaginary past situation, regret.
structures.primary_communicative_function: Talking about how things could have been different.
structures.when_to_use: To express past unreal conditions.
structures.key_variations: Might have, could have.
structures.common_learner_mistakes: Wrong structure, using second conditional for past.
structures.chunk_compatibility: "If I had studied harder, I would have passed.", "I wouldn't have done that."
examples: If we had left earlier, we wouldn't have missed the flight.
She would have helped if you had asked.
If I had known, I would have told you.

structures.structure: Mixed conditional (If + past perfect, would + base)
structures.core_meaning: Past condition with present result.
structures.primary_communicative_function: Explaining a present situation as result of past event.
structures.when_to_use: When a past action affects now.
structures.key_variations: Not always if-clause first.
structures.common_learner_mistakes: Using it for past result only.
structures.chunk_compatibility: "If I had saved more money, I wouldn't be broke now.", "If she had taken the job, she'd be in London."
examples: If you had listened to me, you wouldn't be in this mess.
I would be rich if I had bought that stock.
If he'd been born in Italy, he would speak Italian.

structures.core_meaning: Desire for a different present reality.
structures.when_to_use: To talk about things you want to be different now.
structures.key_variations: I wish I could...; If only...
structures.common_learner_mistakes: Using present after wish (I wish I am...), using would wish.
structures.chunk_compatibility: "I wish I had a bigger house.", "I wish I could swim.", "I wish it were Friday."
examples: I wish I knew the answer.
She wishes she didn't have to work.
We wish we lived near the beach.

structures.core_meaning: Regret about something that happened or didn't happen.
structures.when_to_use: To express regret about past actions.
structures.key_variations: If only...
structures.common_learner_mistakes: Using present perfect or past simple.
structures.chunk_compatibility: "I wish I had studied more.", "I wish you hadn't said that."
examples: I wish I had taken the job.
She wishes she had listened to her mother.
They wish they hadn't moved.

structures.core_meaning: Desire for someone/something to change behavior or for something to happen.
structures.when_to_use: When you are annoyed or want a different future action.
structures.key_variations: I wish it would stop...
structures.common_learner_mistakes: Using with I (I wish I would...) usually not.
structures.chunk_compatibility: "I wish you would listen.", "I wish it would stop raining.", "I wish he wouldn't talk so loudly."
examples: I wish the bus would come.
I wish my neighbors would be quieter.
Do you ever wish people would just leave you alone?

structures.core_meaning: Reporting what someone said, with tense shift.
structures.when_to_use: To tell someone what else someone said.
structures.key_variations: Told + person; present simple -> past simple, will -> would, can -> could, etc.
structures.common_learner_mistakes: Not backshifting, mixing direct speech, pronoun changes missed.
structures.chunk_compatibility: "He said he was tired.", "She told me she would call.", "They said they had finished."
examples: She said (that) she liked the movie.
He told me he had already eaten.
They said they were moving to Canada.

structures.core_meaning: Focus on action/object rather than doer.
structures.primary_communicative_function: When the agent is unknown, unimportant, or obvious.
structures.when_to_use: News, processes, formal English.
structures.key_variations: Modal passives (can be done); present perfect passive (has been done).
structures.common_learner_mistakes: Overusing by-phrase, forgetting be, using intransitive verbs.
structures.chunk_compatibility: "It was made in China.", "English is spoken here.", "The window was broken."
examples: My car was stolen last night.
The report hasn't been finished yet.
This room is cleaned every day.

structures.core_meaning: Arranging for someone else to do something for you.
structures.when_to_use: When you don't do it yourself.
structures.key_variations: Get something done (informal); need something done.
structures.common_learner_mistakes: Thinking it's passive about subject (I had cut my hair vs I had my hair cut).
structures.chunk_compatibility: "I had my hair done.", "She's having her house painted.", "Where do you get your car fixed?"
examples: I need to have my eyes tested.
We had our roof repaired last month.
He's getting his suit cleaned.

structures.core_meaning: Comparing two things (more...).
structures.when_to_use: When two items are different.
structures.key_variations: much/a lot/a bit + comparative; as...as for equality.
structures.essential_vocabulary_slots: Common adjectives (taller, more expensive, better, worse).
structures.common_learner_mistakes: Double comparative (more taller), using as...than, forgetting than.
structures.chunk_compatibility: "I'm taller than you.", "This is more interesting than that.", "It's better than before."
examples: My car is faster than yours.
This book is more difficult than the last one.
She's younger than me.

structures.core_meaning: The highest degree in a group.
structures.primary_communicative_function: Stating the number one in quality.
structures.when_to_use: To describe extremes within a set.
structures.key_variations: One of the + superlative + plural noun.
structures.essential_vocabulary_slots: Extreme adjectives (best, worst, biggest, most beautiful).
structures.common_learner_mistakes: Forgetting "the", using comparative instead of superlative.
structures.chunk_compatibility: "She's the smartest in the class.", "This is the best pizza ever.", "It's the most expensive hotel."
examples: Russia is the largest country.
He's the funniest person I know.
This is the worst day of my life.

structures.core_meaning: Inferior or not equal comparison.
structures.primary_communicative_function: Saying something is less or not equal.
structures.when_to_use: To deny equality.
structures.key_variations: less + adjective + than (formal); as...as affirmative (He's as tall as me).
structures.common_learner_mistakes: Using not so...as (acceptable but less taught), incorrect adjective form.
structures.chunk_compatibility: "It's not as good as I thought.", "She's not as old as her sister."
examples: This phone isn't as expensive as that one.
I'm not as tired as yesterday.
The exam was not as difficult as we expected.

structures.core_meaning: Equality in quality or quantity.
structures.when_to_use: When two things are equal.
structures.key_variations: as much/many as.
structures.common_learner_mistakes: Using than instead of as.
structures.chunk_compatibility: "You're as tall as your dad.", "It's as good as new.", "He can't run as fast as his brother."
examples: She's as smart as her sister.
I work as hard as you.
This cake is not as sweet as the last one. (negative equality)

structures.core_meaning: Checking information or inviting agreement.
structures.when_to_use: After statements, expecting yes/no.
structures.key_variations: Tags with auxiliary (aren't you?, isn't it?, didn't you?, can't you?, shall we?).
structures.common_learner_mistakes: Wrong polarity (You're tired, isn't it?), wrong pronoun.
structures.chunk_compatibility: "You're coming, aren't you?", "It's cold, isn't it?", "You don't know, do you?"
examples: He's a teacher, isn't he?
You won't forget, will you?
They went home, didn't they?
